summary: Nadia reflects on her complex psychological response to sexual misconduct and abuse of power in the tech industry and beyond. She describes how she long prided herself on staying silent about her own experiences, only to realize that silence was a form of self-protection that also protected her abusers. Reading Ronan Farrow's nuanced New Yorker piece about Weinstein broke something open for her, because unlike reductive media portrayals, it captured the messy reality of how victims normalize trauma. She explores why binary media narratives of good vs. evil feel alienating to actual victims, who experience self-doubt, continued relationships with abusers, and internalized shame. Ultimately, she concludes that true power will come not from public confession or silence, but from genuine indifference toward the people who harmed her.
key_points: Victims of misconduct often normalize their experiences through silence, self-blame, and maintaining relationships with abusers, which is a predictable trauma response rather than evidence that nothing happened
Binary media portrayals of sexual misconduct as clear-cut good vs. evil alienate actual victims, whose experiences are tangled with ambiguity, guilt, and self-doubt
Silence is not neutral — it actively bonds victims to their abusers by requiring constant effort to protect secrets and manage encounters
Women often report misconduct as a group not just for stronger evidence, but because collective action shifts the spotlight away from the victim's identity onto the perpetrator's
The essay reframes the conversation from being about specific incidents to the universal psychology of how people on the receiving end of power abuse cope and rationalize
money_quotes: Yes, of course, we all go through these things. Some of us just don't talk about it.
The binary media portrayal is written by people looking for a story, one that requires flattening its characters into a paper battle of good vs. evil.
The declarations of 'we have to do better' (sexual misconduct's version of 'thoughts and prayers').
The transgressions that tend to be widely reported are often, paradoxically, the least vicious.
I've spent my whole life trying to normalize trauma. And part of that process meant staying silent.
Being this quiet, and working this hard to make sure the stories don't get out, means I am only bonding myself even more irrevocably to these people. Love and hate are equal and opposing forces.
When it's a group of women against one person, the spotlight shines on that person alone. Victims don't have to be protagonists in a class-action story, and that's a relief.
This isn't a story about sex, assault, or even harassment. It's the story that accompanies every abuse of power.
key_insight: Silence around abuse of power is not passive indifference but an active, exhausting process of self-protection that paradoxically binds victims more tightly to their abusers.
